reasoning: In Q4 2024—the peak winter demand period—the EU imported around 13.4 billion cubic meters (bcm) of Russian natural gas across all routes (pipeline and LNG). With the expiration of the Russia-Ukraine transit agreement in January 2025, approximately 14-15 bcm/year (or roughly 3.5 bcm/quarter) of pipeline capacity to the EU has been permanently removed. This leaves only the TurkStream pipeline (maximum capacity to Europe of ~15.75 bcm/year) and LNG shipments (historically ~20 bcm/year) as the remaining avenues for Russian gas. Combined, the theoretical maximum capacity via these routes is roughly 35.75 bcm/year, or around 9 bcm per quarter. Even operating at full capacity, Russian imports would fall far short of the 15 bcm quarterly threshold. Furthermore, the EU's REPowerEU policy actively aims to scale down these remaining volumes. Therefore, achieving 15 bcm in any single quarter of 2025 is effectively impossible given current infrastructure and political constraints, aligning with the crowd forecast of 1%. (I am logging 0.05 to align with the forecasting system's bounds.)
